title: FamilyRelationships

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_5 -- "FamilyRelationships".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package -- the parent main.tex in
% this folder provides the preamble.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_5
% title: FamilyRelationships
\def\rowref{def\_3\_5}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_5}

\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
     We then define:
     \begin{enumerate}
         \item The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
         %\item[] \PF{maybe better for sets (???) :The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \lp \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)\rp \sm A.\]
         %    but then  if $v \tuh v \in E$ then $v \notin \Pa^G(\lC v\rC ) \neq \Pa^G(v) \ni v$ ... }
         \item The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
         %\item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
         \item The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
         \item The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } 
             v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
         \item[] The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.
         %\item The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $v$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ collider walk: } 
         %    v \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus w \in G \rC \sm \{v\}.\]
         %    Note: $\lp\Pa^G(v) \cup \Ch^G(v) \cup \Pa^G(\Ch^G(v))\rp \sm \{v\} \ins \MBl^G_d(v)$.
         %\item[] The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \MBl^G_d(v) \sm A.\]
         %\item The $\sigma$-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_\sigma(A):= \MBl^{G^\acy}_d(A), \]
         %    where $G^\acy$ is an acyclification of $G$, a notion defined below in Definition~\refrow{def_3_21}.
     \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. We then define:

  1. The set of parents of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
  2. The set of parents of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
  3. The set of children of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
  4. The set of children of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
  5. The set of siblings of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
  6. The set of ancestors of $v$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
  7. The set of ancestors of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
  8. The set of descendants of $v$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
  9. The set of descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
  10. The set of non-descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
  11. The strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
  12. The (union of) strongly connected components of $A$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
  13. The district of $v$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
  14. The district of $A$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.

lean.name: Pa
PaSet
lean.code:
/-- `PaSet G A` -- the set of *parents* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`: the union of the parent sets of each `v âˆˆ A`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Pa^G(A)`. -/
def PaSet (G : CDMG Î±) (A : Set Î±) : Set Î± :=
  â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, Pa G v

lean.name: Ch
ChSet
lean.code:
/-- `ChSet G A` -- the set of *children* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Ch^G(A)`. -/
def ChSet (G : CDMG Î±) (A : Set Î±) : Set Î± :=
  â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, Ch G v

Anc

LN's implicit precondition (its preamble fixes `v âˆˆ V âŠ† G`). -/
theorem self_mem_Anc {G : CDMG Î±} {v : Î±} (hv : v âˆˆ G) :
    v âˆˆ Anc G v :=

lean.name: AncSet
lean.code:
/-- `AncSet G A` -- the set of *ancestors* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Anc^G(A)`. -/
def AncSet (G : CDMG Î±) (A : Set Î±) : Set Î± :=
  â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, Anc G v

lean.name: subset_Anc_set
lean.code:
/-- LN "Note: `A âŠ† Anc^G(A)`" (def 3.5, item 4). Each
`v âˆˆ A âŠ† G.J âˆª G.V` is its own ancestor via the trivial walk. -/
theorem subset_Anc_set {G : CDMG Î±} {A : Set Î±}
    (hA : A âŠ† G.J âˆª G.V) : A âŠ† AncSet G A := by

lean.name: Desc
self_mem_Desc
lean.code:
/-- LN "Note: `v âˆˆ Desc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 5). Witness:
trivial walk. -/
theorem self_mem_Desc {G : CDMG Î±} {v : Î±} (hv : v âˆˆ G) :
    v âˆˆ Desc G v :=

lean.name: DescSet
lean.code:
/-- `DescSet G A` -- the set of *descendants* of the set `A` in
`G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Desc^G(A)`. -/
def DescSet (G : CDMG Î±) (A : Set Î±) : Set Î± :=
  â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, Desc G v

lean.name: subset_Desc_set
NonDesc
lean.code:
/-- `NonDesc G A` -- the set of *non-descendants* of the set `A`
in `G`: vertices of `G` (i.e. members of `G.J âˆª G.V`) that are
not descendants of any vertex in `A`. Matches the LN's
`\NonDesc^G(A)`. The LN provides no vertex-input variant. -/
def NonDesc (G : CDMG Î±) (A : Set Î±) : Set Î± :=
  (G.J âˆª G.V) \ DescSet G A

lean.name: Sc
lean.code:
/-- `Sc G v` -- the *strongly connected component* of `v` in `G`:
the intersection of the ancestor and descendant sets of `v`.
Matches the LN's `\Sc^G(v)`. Equivalently, the set of `w âˆˆ G`
that lie on a directed walk to `v` and on a directed walk from
`v`. -/
def Sc (G : CDMG Î±) (v : Î±) : Set Î± :=
  Anc G v âˆ© Desc G v

lean.name: self_mem_Sc
lean.code:
/-- LN "Note: `v âˆˆ Sc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 7). Composes
`self_mem_Anc` and `self_mem_Desc`. -/
theorem self_mem_Sc {G : CDMG Î±} {v : Î±} (hv : v âˆˆ G) :
    v âˆˆ Sc G v :=

lean.name: ScSet
lean.code:
/-- `ScSet G A` -- the union of *strongly connected components*
of vertices in `A`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Sc^G(A)`. -/
def ScSet (G : CDMG Î±) (A : Set Î±) : Set Î± :=
  â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, Sc G v

lean.name: subset_Sc_set
Dist
self_mem_Dist
lean.code:
/-- LN "Note: `v âˆˆ Dist^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 8). Witness:
trivial walk, which is vacuously `IsBidirected`. -/
theorem self_mem_Dist {G : CDMG Î±} {v : Î±} (hv : v âˆˆ G) :
    v âˆˆ Dist G v :=

lean.name: DistSet
lean.code:
/-- `DistSet G A` -- the *district* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Dist^G(A)`. -/
def DistSet (G : CDMG Î±) (A : Set Î±) : Set Î± :=
  â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, Dist G v

lean.name: subset_Dist_set

lean_explanation:
All eight family operators in this row live under a fixed ambient context: `Î± : Type*` is the vertex type and `G : CDMG Î±` is the CDMG under discussion, matching the LN's preamble "let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG". Vertex-input variants take `v : Î±` and produce `Set Î±`; set-input variants take `A : Set Î±` and also produce `Set Î±`. The LN's set-builder "$\{w \in G \mid \ldots\}$" maps to `{w | w âˆˆ G âˆ§ â€¦}`, where the membership predicate `w âˆˆ G` is shorthand for `w âˆˆ G.J âˆª G.V` (unfolded by the `CDMG.mem_iff` simp lemma).

Three Mathlib idioms appear repeatedly. First, `â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, f G v` (Mathlib's `Set.iUnionâ‚‚` shape) is the canonical way to write "big union indexed over a set"; every set-input variant is literally `â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, <vertex variant> G v`, mirroring the LN's $\bigcup_{v \in A}$. Second, the `\` operator on `Set Î±` is set-theoretic difference (Mathlib `Set.diff`), used in `NonDesc G A := (G.J âˆª G.V) \ DescSet G A` to encode the LN's $(J \cup V) \setminus \Desc^G(A)$. Third, `âˆ©` is set intersection, used in `Sc G v := Anc G v âˆ© Desc G v` for the LN's $\Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v)$.

The three direct-edge operators `Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib` (items 1-3) each encode a single-edge condition: `w âŸ¶[G] v` for parents, `v âŸ¶[G] w` for children, `v âŸ·[G] w` for siblings (the def_3_2 arrows for LN's $\tuh$ and $\huh$ respectively). The four directed-reachability operators `Anc`, `Desc`, `Sc`, plus the bidirected-reachability `Dist` (items 4-5, 7-8) bundle the `w âˆˆ G` guard with an existential `âˆƒ Ï€ : Walk G w v, Ï€.IsDirected` (or `IsBidirected` for `Dist`), reusing the def_3_4 `Walk` data type and the def_3_4 `WalkPredicates` predicates. `NonDesc` (item 6) is the LN's set complement, with the ambient set being the graph's `G.J âˆª G.V` (not `Set.univ`).

Each LN "Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$" / "$A \ins \Anc^G(A)$" reflexivity line becomes a small theorem (`self_mem_Anc`, `subset_Anc_set`, and analogues for `Desc`, `Sc`, `Dist`). The vertex-reflexivity theorems take `hv : v âˆˆ G` as an explicit hypothesis -- the LN's silent preamble $v \in V$ -- and discharge via the trivial walk `Walk.nil v`, which is vacuously both `IsDirected` and `IsBidirected`. The set-reflexivity theorems take `hA : A âŠ† G.J âˆª G.V` and point each `v âˆˆ A` at itself inside the bigunion.
design_choices:
**The `w âˆˆ G` membership guard is baked into every set-builder, even where redundant.** The LN literally writes "$\{w \in G \mid \ldots\}$", and for the direct-edge operators (parents, children, siblings) the guard is propositionally redundant: a directed edge $w \tuh v$ already forces $w \in G$ via the structural constraint `E âŠ† (J âˆª V) Ã— V`. We keep the guard anyway, because for the walk-based operators (ancestors, descendants, districts) the guard is genuinely load-bearing -- the trivial walk `Walk.nil w` exists for every `w : Î±`, so without the guard every vertex of the ambient type, including ones outside the graph, would land in `Anc G w`. Uniform shape across all eight operators is worth more than minimal-redundancy phrasing here, especially since downstream callers pattern-match against the same `w âˆˆ G âˆ§ _` shape regardless of which operator they invoke.

**Two `def`s per operator -- one vertex-input, one set-input -- except where the LN omits one.** The LN overloads $\Pa^G(\cdot)$ for both a vertex and a set argument; Lean cannot overload by argument shape, so we use the `Set` suffix to disambiguate (`Pa` versus `PaSet`). The LN provides no set-input variant of $\Sib^G$ and no vertex-input variant of $\NonDesc^G$ -- those slots are conspicuously absent. We mirror those omissions strictly: no `SibSet`, no vertex-input `NonDesc G v`. Inventing them would diverge from the LN and introduce decisions the LN never made (e.g. should $\NonDesc^G(v)$ exclude $v$ itself?). Downstream rows that genuinely need such variants can introduce them locally.

**Parents are allowed to lie in `G.J`; children are not.** A directed edge $w \tuh v$ satisfies $w \in J \cup V$ but constrains the *target* $v \in V$ -- so the parent set of an output node can contain input nodes, but the child set is always a subset of `G.V`. We do not restrict the parent set to `G.V`; this asymmetry is load-bearing for chapter 4 (CBN factorisation $P(V \mid J) = \prod_v P(v \mid \Pa^G(v))$, where the input nodes enter the factorisation precisely as parents of output nodes) and chapter 5 (do-calculus surgery on edges incident to $\Pa^G(\cdot)$).

**Bidirected edges are deliberately excluded from parents and children.** A bidirected edge $v \huh w$ makes $v$ a *sibling* of $w$, never a parent or child. The LN encodes this by writing $\tuh$ (directed-only) on the parent/child sides and $\huh$ on the sibling side; we match exactly, with `Pa` and `Ch` using `âŸ¶[G]` (LN's $\tuh$, the directed-edge atom) and `Sib` using `âŸ·[G]` (LN's $\huh$, the bidirected atom). This also lines up cleanly with def_3_3's "into/out of" vocabulary: parents are read off "out of" edges from the parent's perspective, children off "out of" edges from the child's perspective, and siblings off edges with arrowheads at both endpoints.

**Ancestors and descendants are existentially quantified over the def_3_4 `Walk` data type, not a separate inductive reachability relation.** Composing on top of `Walk` immediately reuses every walk operation we already have -- length, support, append, reverse -- and matches the LN's prose phrasing "$\exists$ directed walk from $w$ to $v$". The walk's direction matches the LN: `Anc G v` is the existential over `Walk G w v` (walk starts at the candidate ancestor, ends at $v$); `Desc G v` flips it to `Walk G v w`. We do not define `Desc G v := Anc (reverse G) v` via a graph-reversal trick because the LN does not introduce graph reversal at this stage, and the direct two-`def` encoding is more readable.

**The LN's "Note:" reflexivity lines are encoded as proper theorems, not folded into the definition.** Each "Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$" / "$v \in \Sc^G(v)$" / "$v \in \Dist^G(v)$" line becomes a `self_mem_*` theorem; each "Note: $A \subseteq \cdot$" line becomes a `subset_*_set` theorem. The trivial witness is `Walk.nil v` (vacuously both `IsDirected` and `IsBidirected`). Each carries the LN's implicit preamble precondition (`v âˆˆ G` or `A âŠ† G.J âˆª G.V`) as an explicit hypothesis -- chapter 3's later rows then cite these by name rather than re-deriving the trivial-walk witness each time.

**`NonDesc G A := (G.J âˆª G.V) \ DescSet G A`, with the graph's vertex set as the ambient, not `Set.univ`.** The LN writes the complement against $(J \cup V)$, not against the ambient type $\alpha$. A vertex outside `G.J âˆª G.V` is not a non-descendant -- it simply isn't a vertex of $G$ at all. The set-input-only signature also mirrors the LN exactly: downstream do-calculus and ID-algorithm rows always reason about "non-descendants of some set", never of a single vertex, so a hypothetical `NonDesc G v` would be unused weight.

**`Sc G v := Anc G v âˆ© Desc G v`, not a fresh walk-based primitive.** The LN literally writes the intersection. The strongly-connected-component reflexivity `v âˆˆ Sc^G(v)` then composes from `self_mem_Anc` and `self_mem_Desc`. The set-input variant is `ScSet G A := â‹ƒ v âˆˆ A, Sc G v`; the LN's parenthetical "(union of) strongly connected components" makes the bigunion semantics explicit -- $\Sc^G(A)$ is the union of each $v \in A$'s component, not the smallest single component containing $A$.

**Districts use `Walk.IsBidirected`, not a fresh bidirected-only walk type.** A district is the existential over walks all of whose edges are `bidir`, reusing the def_3_4 walk type with the def_3_4 `IsBidirected` predicate. Bidirected edges are symmetric (`G.L_symm`), so the walk-direction choice `Walk G v w` versus `Walk G w v` is propositionally indifferent, but we match the LN's literal phrasing $v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh w$, starting at $v$. Districts deserve their own file (separate from `Anc`/`Desc`/`Sc`) because later chapters' graphical-separation criteria -- the ID algorithm in chapter 8, FCI completeness in chapter 16 -- treat districts as a primitive equivalence class on `V`, and the directed-reachability file should not pull in district lemmas as a transitive dependency for callers that only need ancestors.
